title: Neuhausen-Nymphenburg

infobox.value: residents | foreigners | area (ha) | res./ha | source and further details

(German pronunciation: [[nɔʏˈhaʊzn̩ ˈnʏmfnbʊʁk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) is a borough of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), the capital of the German state of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"). It was created from the merger of the former boroughs of **Neuhausen** ([Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): **Neihausn**) and **Nymphenburg** in 1992.

outgoing_links.target_title: Maximilian III Joseph, Elector of Bavaria

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [48°9′25″N 11°31′0″E﻿ / ﻿48.15694°N 11.51667°E﻿ / 48.15694; 11.51667](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Neuhausen-Nymphenburg&params=48_9_25_N_11_31_0_E_region:DE-BY_type:city_source:dewiki)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Borough of Munich, Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Image-Schloss_Nymphenburg_Munich_CC_edit3.jpg/500px-Image-Schloss_Nymphenburg_Munich_CC_edit3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Image-Schloss_Nymphenburg_Munich_CC_edit3.jpg)

[Nymphenburg Palace](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Palace "Nymphenburg Palace")

**Neuhausen-Nymphenburg** (German pronunciation: [[nɔʏˈhaʊzn̩ ˈnʏmfnbʊʁk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")) is a borough of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"), the capital of the German state of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"). It was created from the merger of the former boroughs of **Neuhausen** ([Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): **Neihausn**) and **Nymphenburg** in 1992.

## Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuhausen-Nymphenburg&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Luftbild_M%C3%BCnchen_Nymphenburg.jpg/250px-Luftbild_M%C3%BCnchen_Nymphenburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luftbild_M%C3%BCnchen_Nymphenburg.jpg)

Neuhausen-Nymphenburg with the Nymphenburg Palace Park

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_09_%28Karte%29_-_Neuhausen_-_Nymphenburg.png/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_09_%28Karte%29_-_Neuhausen_-_Nymphenburg.png)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_09_(Karte)_-_Neuhausen_-_Nymphenburg.png)

Map of the *Stadtbezirk 09*

Nymphenburg borders [Obermenzing](/wiki/Obermenzing "Obermenzing") in the north-west, [Pasing](/wiki/Pasing "Pasing") in the south-west, [Moosach](/wiki/Moosach "Moosach") in the north and Neuhausen in the south-east.

The borough 09 ranges from the Mars-field at the inner edge of town to the [Nymphenburg Palace](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Palace "Nymphenburg Palace") in the west and extends from the south part of the Olympic Park (including the [Tollwood Summer festival](/wiki/Tollwood_Festival "Tollwood Festival") area and the [East-West Peace Church](/wiki/East-West_Peace_Church "East-West Peace Church")) over the villa colony in [Gern](/wiki/Gern "Gern") to the railway tracks.

## History and description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuhausen-Nymphenburg&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History and description")]

Neuhausen is a very quiet and calm residential area. It counts as one of the most exclusive and expensive boroughs in Munich.

Typical of the borough is its mix of different urban areas. Around the end of the 19th Century a prestigious residential neighborhood was built in the palace's vicinity, where numerous examples of late nineteenth century architecture, like the villa colonies *Neuwittelsbach* and *Gern* can be found.

In 1890 the borough Neuhausen was unincorporated, which had already been a prosperous district. It was dominated by residential and office houses in a closed, dense block-building style. Along the Arnulfroad and its side streets, these are often cooperative apartment buildings such as the partly under preservation attempt-settlement of the Bavarian Post and Telegraph Association and the village of Neuhausen. In the north of the Rotkreuzplatz there are more villas and town houses of the early days.

In the south along the railway tracks there are partly dominating large-scale commercial used buildings. Center and urban hub of the neighborhood of the Rotkreuzplatz. Some well-preserved buildings from the founder and inter-war period with the rich variety of green spaces give Neuhausen a high quality of living. While the old quarters in the vicinity of Nymphenburgerstreet and Blutenburgstreet are home to upper middle class for some time, the sometimes less sumptuously executed turn of the century buildings offer in the vicinity of Schulstreet and Donnersbergerstreet housing for various population groups. But has also kept the so-called gentrification catchment by road trains and remodeled buildings were renovated. In addition, there were numerous conversions take place in condominiums and shops were once easy to architectural firms, alternative shops, bars and restaurants. For this reason, the City Council in 2006 extended the statute for preservation of this neighborhood, to counteract the displacement of ancestral inhabitants.

On 1 January 1891 the independent [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") Nymphenburg was incorporated to the city of Munich.

It is named after the [Nymphenburg Palace](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Palace "Nymphenburg Palace"), former summer residence of the [Bavarian kings](/wiki/King_of_Bavaria "King of Bavaria"). Today the castle along with the [Nymphenburg Palace Park](/wiki/Badenburg "Badenburg") is one of the most popular sights of Munich.

The in 1747 by Elector [Max III. Joseph](/wiki/Maximilian_III_Joseph,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian III Joseph, Elector of Bavaria") founded [Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Porcelain_Manufactory "Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory"), the [Royal Stables Museum](/w/index.php?title=Royal_Stables_Museum&action=edit&redlink=1 "Royal Stables Museum (page does not exist)") of Nymphenburg and the [Museum of Man and Nature](/wiki/Museum_of_Man_and_Nature "Museum of Man and Nature") is located next to the palace. On the Nymphenburg Palace Park also borders the [Botanical Garden](/wiki/Botanical_Garden "Botanical Garden"), which entrance is near the Bavarian State Office of Weights and Measures. The [Hirschgarten](/wiki/Hirschgarten "Hirschgarten") and the [Olympic Park](/wiki/Olympic_Park "Olympic Park") complement the diverse range of parks and recreational areas. Because the borough is connected by the motorway [A8](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_8 "Bundesautobahn 8") and the ring road it is charged by high traffic volumes. Neuhausen-Nymphenburg has about 90,000 inhabitants, according to Ramersdorf-Perlach the second highest population of the boroughs of Munich. In the north of Neuhausen, between the borough is Gern and Moosach-Nederling, in the northeastern the borough of Ebenau.

The jobs of the district are in addition to trade and service sector largely in the public sector. Apart from the branch of [Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn"), the [Bundeswehr](/wiki/Bundeswehr "Bundeswehr")'s administrative center, the [Bavarian State Criminal Police Office](/wiki/Landeskriminalamt "Landeskriminalamt"), and numerous hospitals, such as the Red Cross Hospital, the German Heart Center, the Brothers of Mercy Hospital and the Third Order Hospital there are also major social institutions in the borough.

The proportion of foreigners Nymphenburg is low, in Neuhausen it is average. Regarding the age distribution Neuhausen is the younger of the two districts.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuhausen-Nymphenburg&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Politics")]

In Munich each borough has a Borough Committee (*Bezirksausschuss* BA). The Borough Committees are elected by local citizens in municipal elections. The Borough Committee consists 39 members. The election of the Borough Committee of 2 March 2008 yielded the following results:

* [SPD](/wiki/Sozialdemokratische_Partei_Deutschlands "Sozialdemokratische Partei Deutschlands") 16 seats – 41,4%
* [CSU](/wiki/Christlich-Soziale_Union_in_Bayern "Christlich-Soziale Union in Bayern") 10 seats – 26,6%
* [Bündnis 90/Die Grünen](/wiki/B%C3%BCndnis_90/Die_Gr%C3%BCnen "Bündnis 90/Die Grünen") 9 seats – 20,1%
* [FDP](/wiki/Free_Democratic_Party_(Germany) "Free Democratic Party (Germany)") 3 seats – 7,6%
* [David contra Goliath/ÖDP](/wiki/%C3%96kologisch-Demokratische_Partei "Ökologisch-Demokratische Partei") 2 seats – 4,3%

Chairman of the BA is Ingeborg Staudenmeyer (SPD). The Vice-Chairmen are Roland Zintl (Greens) and Immoi Scheibel (CSU). The Greens and the ÖDP provide a common fraction.

The municipality is represented in the City Council by Oliver Belik (SPD) and Councilwoman Elizabeth Schmucker (CSU).
[[1]](#cite_note-Wahl-1)

## Celebrities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Neuhausen-Nymphenburg&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Celebrities")]

According to legend, Neuhausen was christianised by [Blessed Winthir](/w/index.php?title=Blessed_Winthir&action=edit&redlink=1 "Blessed Winthir (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Winthir "de:Winthir")] in the 8th century.

* Author [Alfred Andersch](/wiki/Alfred_Andersch "Alfred Andersch")
* Author [Manfred Bieler](/w/index.php?title=Manfred_Bieler&action=edit&redlink=1 "Manfred Bieler (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manfred_Bieler "de:Manfred Bieler")]
* Historian [Karl Bosl](/wiki/Karl_Bosl "Karl Bosl")
* Architect [Hans Döllgast](/w/index.php?title=Hans_D%C3%B6llgast&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hans Döllgast (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hans_D%C3%B6llgast "de:Hans Döllgast")]
* Toolmaker [Anton Drexler](/wiki/Anton_Drexler "Anton Drexler")[[2]](#cite_note-2)
* Actor [Helmut Fischer](/wiki/Helmut_Fischer "Helmut Fischer")
* Landscape painter [Max Haushofer](/wiki/Max_Haushofer "Max Haushofer") (1811–1866)
* [Ellis Kaut](/wiki/Ellis_Kaut "Ellis Kaut"), developer of [Pumuckl](/wiki/Pumuckl "Pumuckl")
* [Oskar von Miller](/wiki/Oskar_von_Miller "Oskar von Miller"), founder of [Deutschen Museums](/wiki/Deutsches_Museum "Deutsches Museum")
* Sculptor [Karl Knappe](/w/index.php?title=Karl_Knappe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Karl Knappe (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Karl_Knappe "de:Karl Knappe")]
* Soccer-player [Philipp Lahm](/wiki/Philipp_Lahm "Philipp Lahm") (born 1983)
* Composer [Carl Orff](/wiki/Carl_Orff "Carl Orff") (1895–1982)
* Journalist [Ponkie](/w/index.php?title=Ponkie&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ponkie (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ponkie "de:Ponkie")]
* [Lilo Ramdohr](/wiki/Lilo_Ramdohr "Lilo Ramdohr") (born 1913), who lived as Lilo Berndl from 1941 until 1944 in Nymphenburg, where in May 1942 cartoons with leaflets of the [White Rose](/wiki/Wei%C3%9Fe_Rose "Weiße Rose") had been highjacked by [Alexander Schmorell](/wiki/Alexander_Schmorell "Alexander Schmorell")[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* Poet [Eugen Roth](/wiki/Eugen_Roth "Eugen Roth")
* Author [Augustin Souchy](/wiki/Augustin_Souchy "Augustin Souchy")
* Operatic baritone and voice teacher [Hermann Wiedemann](/wiki/Hermann_Wiedemann "Hermann Wiedemann") (1879–1944)
